zones.name: LLM Client Core
zones.description: The complete vendor-neutral LLM client library: provider interface, registry, session management, Claude and Codex adapters, auth, config, token usage, process execution, and shared CLI utilities.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Contains 82% of project files (36/44) — may be too broad, consider splitting

The single-zone topology means all 36 source files are tightly interconnected by import — this is consistent with a foundation package that is designed to have high internal cohesion and zero upstream dependencies.
The public.ts barrel re-exports the entire surface area cleanly, providing a single auditable entry point for downstream consumers (rex, hench, sourcevision, web).

The zone mixes two distinct concerns: LLM provider infrastructure (api-provider, cli-provider, codex-cli-provider, provider-interface, provider-registry, provider-session) and general CLI/process utilities (exec, help-format, suggest, output, project-dirs). As the package grows, these could be split into sub-zones to improve discoverability.
Perfect cohesion score (1.0) confirms all 36 files are fully interconnected within the zone — appropriate for a shared foundation package with a single public API surface.
Zero coupling score reflects the architectural invariant that llm-client imports no sibling packages — it is the DAG root, as documented in public.ts.

General-purpose utilities (exec.ts, help-format.ts, suggest.ts, output.ts) are co-located with LLM-specific infrastructure; if these utilities are consumed by orchestration scripts rather than LLM consumers, extracting them into a separate utility zone would improve single-responsibility clarity.

llm-client-core is the sole hub in a hub-and-spoke topology: package-config governs its build shape, llm-client-tests consumes its exports, and no sibling packages feed into it — a clean DAG leaf from the monorepo's perspective and a clean hub from the intra-package perspective.

Only 5 of 36 source files in llm-client-core are directly exercised by unit-test imports, leaving 86% of the zone's import surface untested at the unit level — provider, auth, exec, and session modules depend entirely on integration or e2e coverage.

86% of llm-client-core files (31/36) have no corresponding cross-zone import from the test zone, concentrating unit-test risk on the provider stack and execution layer — the most behaviorally complex parts of the package.

llm-client-core acts as the single hub for all intra-package cross-zone flows: it is consumed by llm-client-tests and shaped by package-config, with no reciprocal imports — a clean spoke-to-hub dependency direction.

createLLMClient() in llm-client.ts is typed to return ClaudeClient (Claude-specific) rather than LLMProvider (vendor-neutral), meaning the 'vendor-neutral factory' leaks the Claude type to all callers — the abstraction is incomplete at the type level.

CompletionRequest in types.ts includes two CLI-only fields (cliFlags, timeoutMs) with doc comments acknowledging they are provider-specific — these fields pollute a shared cross-package contract type with implementation details from one provider.

There are two independent vendor dispatch paths: createLLMClient() (llm-client.ts) and ProviderRegistry.getActiveProvider() (provider-registry.ts). Both resolve vendor → create client from LLMConfig, but they do not delegate to each other, so adding a new vendor requires updating both paths.

The codex provider in createDefaultRegistry() wraps createCodexCliClient (which returns ClaudeClient) with an inline shim to satisfy the LLMProvider interface. The comment acknowledges this is temporary tech debt — but the shim sits inside the registry, which should contain no provider logic, blurring the registry's role as a pure dispatch table.

spawnTool() and spawnManaged() in exec.ts duplicate the SIGTERM → SIGKILL escalation timer pattern verbatim (approximately 15 lines each). The shared constant KILL_ESCALATION_MS was extracted but the surrounding logic was not, leaving the two implementations able to diverge silently.

exec.ts co-locates git-specific synchronous helpers (getCurrentHead, getCurrentBranch) with general process execution utilities. These are domain-specific operations that couple the process module to git semantics.

createLLMClient() returns ClaudeClient instead of LLMProvider. Callers of the 'vendor-neutral' factory receive a Claude-typed value, which prevents swapping vendors transparently and exposes Claude-specific interface members (e.g. mode: AuthMode) to vendor-agnostic consumers. Should return LLMProvider to honour the abstraction boundary.

CompletionRequest (types.ts) contains CLI-provider-specific fields cliFlags and timeoutMs. This shared contract type, imported by all domain packages, is contaminated with implementation details from one provider backend. CLI-specific options should move to CliProviderOptions and be excluded from the vendor-neutral request type.

Two independent vendor selection paths exist: createLLMClient() in llm-client.ts and ProviderRegistry.getActiveProvider() in provider-registry.ts. They implement the same vendor-resolution logic (config.vendor ?? 'claude') without delegating to each other, so a new vendor must be registered in both places to be fully supported.

The codex entry in createDefaultRegistry() constructs an inline LLMProvider adapter around createCodexCliClient rather than the codex provider implementing LLMProvider directly. Registry code is supposed to be a pure dispatch table with no provider logic; the shim belongs in codex-cli-provider.ts.

spawnTool() and spawnManaged() each contain a near-verbatim copy of the SIGTERM→SIGKILL escalation timer block (~15 lines). Only the constant KILL_ESCALATION_MS is shared. The duplicated logic should be extracted into a private helper to prevent the two paths from drifting apart on future changes.

Naming asymmetry across provider files: 'api-provider.ts' and 'cli-provider.ts' have no vendor prefix while 'codex-cli-provider.ts' carries an explicit vendor prefix — implying api-provider and cli-provider are Claude-specific by convention but not by name, which misleads readers about vendor scope.

'create-client.ts' uses verb-object (action) naming while every other file in the zone uses noun naming (api-provider, auth, config, exec) — the factory module breaks the implicit naming convention of the package.

'suggest.ts' is an opaque name inside an LLM client package; without reading the file, it is unclear whether it provides LLM prompt suggestions, CLI autocomplete, or something else — the name does not self-document its role.

'provider-interface.ts' uses a TypeScript concept ('interface') as a filename suffix rather than the domain concept it represents; conventional TypeScript naming would be 'provider.ts' or 'llm-provider.ts', reserving the file for the type definition rather than advertising its implementation form.

'llm-types.ts' and 'types.ts' coexist in the same zone, creating ambiguity about which file owns which type contracts — without reading both, consumers cannot determine the difference between 'llm types' and generic 'types', risking duplication or misrouting of new type definitions.

Rename 'api-provider.ts' → 'claude-api-provider.ts' and 'cli-provider.ts' → 'claude-cli-provider.ts' to match the 'codex-cli-provider.ts' naming pattern; all three are vendor-specific and the asymmetric naming implies api-provider/cli-provider are vendor-neutral when they are not.

Consolidate 'llm-types.ts' and 'types.ts' into a single file or establish an explicit naming rule distinguishing them (e.g. 'types.ts' for vendor-neutral shared types, 'llm-types.ts' for provider-facing contracts); coexistence without a documented split invites accidental duplication of type definitions.
Rename 'suggest.ts' to a name that communicates its domain role (e.g. 'config-suggest.ts' or 'value-suggest.ts') — 'suggest' is too generic and does not self-document within an LLM client package.

Rename 'provider-interface.ts' to 'llm-provider.ts' — the filename suffix '-interface' describes the TypeScript construct rather than the domain concept, inconsistent with every other file in the zone which names the domain entity.
[call graph] 443 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 51 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

zones.name: LLM Client Tests
zones.description: Unit tests covering the five public-API entry points: config, JSON serialization, project-config overrides, CLI typo suggestion, and shared types.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected

The five test files map 1:1 to the five declared entry points in the src zone (config.ts, json.ts, project-config.ts, suggest.ts, types.ts), demonstrating disciplined test coverage alignment with the public API.
All cross-zone imports (5) flow from tests → src, which is the correct unidirectional direction; no test utilities leak back into production code.

The test zone covers utility and config modules well, but provider-facing modules (api-provider, cli-provider, codex-cli-provider, auth, token-usage) have no corresponding test files visible here — integration or e2e tests likely cover these, but unit-level coverage for provider logic should be verified.

1:1 mapping between test files and declared src entry points indicates the test suite is scoped to the package's public API boundary rather than implementation internals — a sustainable, refactor-friendly pattern.

No test files exist for the provider stack (api-provider, cli-provider, codex-cli-provider) or the exec/auth modules within this zone; confirm these are covered by integration tests to avoid coverage gaps in the most complex parts of the package.
Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling in both zones means the test boundary is clean — tests import only from src, and src has no test-layer dependencies.

The test zone's 5 cross-zone imports cover only utility and config modules (config.ts, json.ts, project-config.ts, suggest.ts, types.ts), forming a narrow slice of the public API surface; the provider-facing half of llm-client-core's exports has no unit-level test zone attachment at all.

llm-client-tests imports exclusively from utility/config modules in llm-client-core, creating a structural split: utility logic is unit-tested via cross-zone imports while provider and execution logic is not — any regression in those paths requires integration-level detection.

The test zone's perfect cohesion score (1.0) is a structural artifact of its small size (5 files) and shared imports from the same 5 src modules — not evidence of intentional mutual coupling between tests; cohesion metrics on pure test zones are not meaningful and should not be interpreted as a quality signal.

No test file in the zone covers 'types.ts' directly, despite 'types.ts' being a shared cross-package contract type imported by rex, hench, and sourcevision — regressions in the vendor-neutral request/response type surface have no unit-level guard within this package.

Add a unit test file for 'types.ts' (CompletionRequest, CompletionResponse, LLMProvider interface shape) — this is the cross-package contract type consumed by every downstream package, and it currently has no unit-level regression guard in the test zone.
[call graph] 0 internal calls, 51 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 0, coupling: 1)

zones.name: Package Config
zones.description: Top-level package manifest and build tooling configuration for the llm-client package.
insights: Isolated files — no import edges between 3 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
These three files define the package identity, TypeScript compilation target, and test runner setup — a stable, low-churn configuration layer.
No production logic lives here; changes are primarily driven by dependency upgrades or build tooling shifts.
Consider collocating any `.n-dx.json` overrides or `.npmrc` files here to keep package-level config centralized.
Zero cohesion and coupling scores reflect that these files share no import relationships with each other — expected and correct for a config-only zone.
The zone contains exactly the right files: package.json, tsconfig.json, and vitest.config.ts form a minimal, complete build-config surface with no extraneous entries.
This zone is stable by design; frequent changes here would be a signal that build tooling churn is bleeding into the project.

The package-config zone exerts governance over both llm-client-core and llm-client-tests through build tooling (tsconfig.json, vitest.config.ts) without any import-level coupling — a non-import dependency invisible to the zone graph but architecturally real.

package-config governs llm-client-core and llm-client-tests via build tooling (tsconfig paths, vitest globals) with zero import edges — this out-of-band coupling is structurally sound but invisible to import-graph analysis tools.

The zone name 'package-config' does not follow the 'llm-client-' prefix convention used by the other two zones ('llm-client-core', 'llm-client-tests'), creating a naming inconsistency in the zone taxonomy itself.
Rename zone to 'llm-client-config' to match the 'llm-client-*' prefix convention used by the other two zones; inconsistent zone naming makes tooling output harder to scan at a glance.
Size imbalance: largest zone has 36 files vs smallest with 3 — uneven decomposition

The two-zone structure (config + src) with a clean test overlay is well-suited to a foundation package: all production logic lives in one highly-cohesive zone, and the test zone exercises exactly the public API surface.
The single outbound cross-zone import direction (unit → src, 5 imports) confirms no circular dependencies and no test-layer contamination of production code.

As the provider roster expands (e.g. Gemini, Bedrock), consider whether the monolithic src zone should be partitioned into provider-specific sub-zones to keep the import graph navigable and make vendor-specific changes easier to scope.
llm-client achieves zero outbound coupling to any sibling package, correctly fulfilling its role as the DAG root of the n-dx monorepo dependency hierarchy.

The package exposes a wide public API surface (60+ exports in public.ts) spanning LLM providers, process execution, CLI utilities, and config — consumers should import only what they need to avoid inadvertent coupling to unrelated utilities.

No integration or e2e test files are present in this zone snapshot; verify that provider-level behavior (auth detection, API vs CLI dispatch, token usage parsing) is covered by tests at the integration layer to guard against regressions in the most critical package in the monorepo.
File "tests/unit/api-provider.test.ts" has 100% of import neighbors in src/ but lives in tests/unit/ — consider relocating

The three-zone structure forms a strict DAG with a single import direction (tests → core) and zero cycles — the inter-zone relationship topology is maximally simple and correct for a foundation package.

The non-import dependency from package-config to both other zones (via tsconfig and vitest config) is the only 'hidden' coupling in the package; documenting it explicitly would close the gap between the import graph view and the true build-time dependency graph.

The sparse test-zone coverage (5 import edges across 36 source files) is the primary inter-zone risk: it means the package's cross-zone contract is only partially validated at the unit level, and consumers of llm-client-core's provider APIs have weaker local feedback loops than consumers of its utility APIs.
Hub-and-spoke topology with llm-client-core as hub: package-config governs via build tooling, llm-client-tests consumes via imports — no cycles, no bidirectional edges, structurally sound.

Inter-zone test coverage asymmetry: 5 utility modules are unit-tested via cross-zone imports while 31 provider/exec/auth modules are not — the boundary between unit-tested and untested zones tracks the utility/provider split rather than any explicit API boundary.

The llm-client-core zone contains two overlapping abstraction layers for vendor-neutral client creation (createLLMClient + ProviderRegistry) that were likely added at different times without reconciling the design; the registry is the more extensible pattern and createLLMClient should delegate to it rather than duplicate the dispatch logic.

The vendor-neutral promise of the package is partially broken at the type level: the public API surface exports both LLMProvider (generic) and ClaudeClient (Claude-specific), and the primary factory (createLLMClient) returns the Claude-specific type — downstream consumers who want true vendor neutrality must use the registry path instead, which is less discoverable.

The llm-client package's vendor-neutral abstraction (LLMProvider, createLLMClient, loadLLMConfig) is structurally incomplete: the factory returns a Claude-specific type, the request type carries CLI-only fields, and the codex adapter requires a shim rather than implementing the interface natively. These three gaps together mean the 'vendor-neutral foundation' is Claude-first with codex bolted on rather than genuinely provider-agnostic.

The zone graph has three nodes but only one import edge direction (tests → core); 'package-config' is connected to the other two zones only via build-tooling, meaning the import graph and the build graph tell different stories about the package's internal topology — a risk for tools that reason only from imports.

Across all three zones, there is no file responsible for documenting or enforcing which exports are considered stable public API vs internal — 'public.ts' serves as a de-facto stability boundary but carries no machine-readable or human-readable stability annotations, leaving consumers without a signal when they reach for an internal module directly.

Establish an explicit naming rule for test files that distinguishes unit tests covering public API surface ('types.test.ts', 'config.test.ts') from those covering implementation details — currently the test zone covers only 5 of 22 src files with no documented rationale for the selection, making it unclear whether the remaining 17 are intentionally untested at unit level or simply missed.
[call graph] 51 calls: "llm-client-tests" → "llm-client-core"
